\section{Related Work and Conclusion}
\label{sec:concl}

Runtime bytecode verification at module load time is standard for typed managed virtual machines: the Java Virtual Machine~\cite{jvm} and the Common Language Runtime~\cite{clr} both run a verifier at class-load time to check well-formedness, typing, and stack discipline before bytecode is executed, and the sibling Move VMs deployed elsewhere --- notably the original Diem MoveVM~\cite{OLD_MOVE_LANG} and Sui's variant --- inherit Move's bytecode verifier.
None of these platforms maintains a \emph{second} layer of runtime safety checks during execution: once the verifier admits a module, its dynamic semantics is trusted.
At the opposite end of the spectrum, dynamically typed languages such as JavaScript or Python perform the runtime type checks of \S\ref{sec:type}, but lack the \emph{first} static layer.
The Ethereum Virtual Machine~\cite{ethereum,kevm} and Solana's SVM sit in a third regime: their bytecode is untyped (a stack of $256$-bit words for the EVM; raw memory regions for the SVM), so neither a type-based static verifier nor a runtime type discipline applies.
Aptos's combination of a static bytecode verifier with a complementary runtime layer is, to our knowledge, unique among production VMs and goes beyond the safety guarantees of any other smart-contract platform.

The dynamic reference safety checks (DRSC) we describe are novel.
The closest related works on Tree Borrows and Stacked Borrows~\cite{stackedborrows,treeborrows} (TB/SB) propose to clarify Rust's unsafe aliasing rules.
DRSC is designed not to have false positives over the static verifier, whereas TB/SB do not share that goal.
TB/SB can also tolerate two orders or more of magnitude in performance overhead: they not expected to be run in production or huge fuzzing workloads.
Unlike DRSC, they maintain provenance genealogies among references and are not function-modular.
Also see the difference in Move and Rust's references in \S\ref{sec:move}.

\george{3 sentences below seem a bit out of context, like future work, maybe cut?}
The dynamic semantics is also interesting as a reference model for what reference safety means, as most static analyses are over-approximations determined by the particular method chosen, and a canonical semantics does not exist.
More work is needed to fully formalize and categorize dynamic reference safety.

In addition to providing a second layer of defense against latent bugs in the static verifier, the runtime checks also have a valuable role in fuzzing the VM: they provide a semantic and precise oracle for finding bugs in the static verifier.

Deferring runtime checks off the execution path and replaying a recorded trace to perform them later is a well-known idea.
Decoupled dynamic analysis~\cite{aftersight,shadowreplica,straighttaint} and offline runtime verification~\cite{rvsurvey} move expensive monitoring onto spare cores.
Asynchronous checks we present differ in that the parallel blockchain execution model itself supplies the idle capacity (from workers waiting on read-write conflicts) and runs all checks online so offending transaction can be detected automatically.

\george{should be tightened, and connected to novel ref-checks work}
In this paper we presented the runtime safety checks that Aptos uses in production as a second line of defense to guard against Move bytecode verifier bugs.
We presented examples of real verifier bugs surfaced during addition of new features to the Move language which were all caught by the runtime verifier.
We presented two techniques to reduce the performance cost of such checks which apply under the blockchain setting:
1) trusted code waiving the checks, agreed by the blockchain governance protocol and 2) asynchronous type checks using idle capacity in parallel transaction execution engines.
Our results show that runtime checks introduce not more than 10\% overhead on production workloads on the Aptos blockchain.
